title: The Feast of Christ the King
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: November 22, 2020

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Feast of Christ the King, the final Sunday of Ordinary Time before Advent begins on November 29, 2020. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have included Matthew 25:31-46 (the Judgment of the Nations), Ezekiel 34:11-16, and 20, Psalm 100, and 1 Corinthians 15:20-28, all emphasizing Christ's ultimate sovereignty and the separation of sheep from goats. This feast commemorates the kingship of Christ over all creation and traditionally calls the church to consider kingdom values of justice and mercy.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church remained in the post-General Convention 2018 period, having not yet convened for its triennial General Convention (scheduled for July 2021 in Baltimore, delayed from 2020). The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Konde, was operating during the COVID-19 pandemic with limited in-person worship; St. John's Cathedral itself was providing hybrid services after having suspended indoor gathering in spring 2020. No major denominational controversies or bishop elections were active in this specific week.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing fall weather with temperatures in the 50s-60s Fahrenheit that week. Colorado had just completed its general election on November 3, 2020 (held three weeks prior), and the state was finalizing election results and certifications. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, where St. John's Cathedral is located, continued operating under Denver's COVID-19 precautions, with many businesses still adapting to reduced capacity and remote operations. No major weather events or local crises occurred in the immediate week before this sermon.

NATIONAL NEWS

The dominant national story was the post-election period following the November 3 presidential election, with Joe Biden projected as president-elect but President Trump disputing results and pursuing legal challenges across multiple states. The COVID-19 pandemic continued to surge ahead of the winter months, with cases and hospitalizations rising significantly nationwide. The death toll from COVID-19 had just exceeded 250,000 Americans as of mid-November 2020. Thanksgiving was four days away (November 26), and public health officials were warning Americans against traveling for the holiday.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The global COVID-19 death toll had exceeded 1.2 million, with Europe experiencing a severe second wave and entering renewed lockdowns in late November. Brazil continued to suffer from one of the world's highest COVID-19 case counts. The U.S. election outcome was being closely watched internationally, with foreign governments and media analyzing implications of the transition of power.

CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was fractious and anxious: Americans were deeply divided over the election outcome, pandemic fatigue was widespread after nine months of disruption, and uncertainty dominated regarding public health, economic recovery, and social stability. Thanksgiving and Christmas were approaching amid concerns about family gatherings during a pandemic surge. The cultural moment was marked by political polarization, health anxiety, economic precarity for many, and a sense of institutional stress. Churches nationwide were grappling with how to maintain community and hope amid closure and division.
